question:
Will the Federal Reserve's Bank Prime Loan Rate, the rate posted by a majority of top US commercial banks have increased by 2026-06-28 as compared to its value on 2026-06-21? Resolution criteria: Resolves to the value found at https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/DPRIME once the data is published. Background: The notes from the release: For questions on the data, please contact the data source: https://www.federalreserve.gov/apps/ContactUs/feedback.aspx?refurl=/releases/h15/% For questions on FRED functionality, please contact: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/contactus/. The notes from the series: data source (https://www.federalreserve.gov/apps/ContactUs/feedback.aspx?refurl=/releases/h15/%). For questions on FRED functionality, please contact us here (https://fred.stlouisfed.org/contactus/).

. Additional background of the series: 1. the units of the series: Percent. 2. the seasonal adjustments of the series: Not Seasonally Adjusted 3. the update frequency: Daily

evidences.title: Federal Reserve Statistical Release H.15 - Grokipedia
evidences.content: facilities, which may influence comparability with pre-2008 data without excluding affected paper.(#ref-1)](#ref-1) The bank prime loan rate is collected from the posted rates of a majority of the top 25 insured U.S.-chartered commercial banks, ranked by assets in domestic offices, serving as a benchmark for short-term business lending. This aggregation reflects consensus pricing among major lenders, updated as banks adjust their base rates in response to market dynamics.(#ref-1)](#ref-1) The primary credit rate for the discount window is directly set by the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, as part of the Federal Reserve's primary credit program effective January 9, 2003, which replaced the prior adjustment credit rate. This administratively determined rate applies to short-term loans [...] and quoted on a discount basis where applicable. Financial paper insured by the FDIC's Temporary Liquidity Guarantee Program is included, and rates after September 19, 2008, may reflect effects of Federal Reserve liquidity facilities, making them not directly comparable to prior periods.(#ref-1)](#ref-1) Lending rates from commercial banks and the Federal Reserve's discount facility are also...

evidences.title: Federal Reserve Statistical Release H.15 - Grokipedia
evidences.content: The Federal Reserve Statistical Release H.15, titled "Selected Interest Rates (Daily)," is a publication by the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System that provides daily data on key interest rates for various financial instruments, serving as a primary tool for monitoring monetary policy and financial market conditions.(#ref-1)](#ref-1) Released every weekday at 4:15 p.m. Eastern Time—except on federal holidays or when the Board is closed—it compiles yields expressed as percent per annum, covering instruments such as the effective federal funds rate, commercial paper rates for nonfinancial and financial issuers (in 1-, 2-, and 3-month terms), the bank prime loan rate, and the discount window primary credit rate.(#ref-1)](#ref-1) Additionally, it includes rates for U.S. [...] The Federal Reserve Statistical Release H.15 is published daily from Monday through Friday at 4:15 p.m. Eastern Time, providing timely updates on selected interest rates to support economic analysis and market monitoring.(#ref-1)](#ref-1) This consistent schedule ensures reliability, with each release incorporating data from recent business days to reflect current market conditions. Postings do no...

evidences.content: Release Information #### Description For the U.S., the Federal Reserve Board's "Selected Interest Rates (H.15)" statistical release contains daily interest rates for selected monetary policy, U.S. Treasury (secondary market), private money market, and capital market instruments. Most of the data are republished from other sources, such as the U.S. Treasury and Depository Trust Company. #### Properties Measurements: + Percent per annum (% p.a.) + Percentage (%) Adjustment: Not seasonally adjusted (NSA) Native frequencies: Business-daily, weekly, monthly Start date: As early as 1919m1 #### Methodology Weekly, monthly, and annual figures are averages of business days unless otherwise noted. ## Definitions [...] # United States - Interest rates: Bank Prime Loan #### United States: Interest rates: Bank Prime Loan | | | --- | | Mnemonic | IRPRIMEM.IUSA | | Unit | % p.a., NSA | | Adjustments | Not Seasonally Adjusted | | Monthly | 0 % | | Data | Jun 2026 | 6.75 | | May 2026 | 6.75 | #### Series Information | | | --- | | Source | U.S. Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (FRB)") | | Release | [H.15 Selected Interest Rates [D, W, M]]( "H.15 Selected Interest Rates [D, W, M]")...

evidences.title: What is the prime rate, and who borrows at that interest rate?
evidences.content: Sources of prime rate data So, whether you are a consumer borrowing against your home or on your credit card with a floating interest rate like the prime rate, or a business-owner taking out a commercial loan priced off of the prime rate, knowing what the prime rate is and how it behaves over time is important to your financial well being. You can find historical information on the prime rate and other interest rates at the H.15 historical index page on the Federal Reserve Board’s website. Additionally, you may go to FRED, the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis’s database website and download prime rate data or create a chart showing the behavior of the prime rate over a period of several years. Data Consumer Credit (G.19) Selected Interest Rates (Weekly) (H.15) [...] Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco LinkedIn Facebook X YouTube Instagram Threads # What is the prime rate, and who borrows at that interest rate? June 1, 2005 This is an interesting question for many borrowers. The prime rate is a “reference or base rate” that banks use to set the price or interest rate on many of their commercial loans and some of their consumer loan product...

evidences.title: The Fed - What is the prime rate, and does the Federal Reserve set ...
evidences.content: The prime rate is an interest rate determined by individual banks. It is often used as a reference rate (also called the base rate) for many types of loans, including loans to small businesses and credit card loans. On its H.15 statistical release, "Selected Interest Rates," the Board reports the prime rate posted by the majority of the largest twenty-five banks. Although the Federal Reserve has no direct role in setting the prime rate, many banks choose to set their prime rates based partly on the target level of the federal funds rate--the rate that banks charge each other for short-term loans--established by the Federal Open Market Committee. #### Related Information Selected Interest Rates -- H.15 ###### Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System

The prime rate is a “reference or base rate” that banks use to set the price or interest rate on many of their commercial loans and some of their consumer loan products. The prime rate tracks fairly closely with other short-term interest rates, such as the overnight federal funds rate; this relationship is illustrated in Chart I. Back to top ##### Where can I get weekly and monthly averages for rates on the H.15? The weekly and monthly averages are the average of all non-missing values during the given time period. If you prefer not to calculate them yourself, you can access them from the Data Download Program (DDP) at: In the column on the right, you can "Select a preformatted data package". From there, you can select one of the packages with weekly or monthly averages. More help for the DDP is available at: [...] Alternatively, you can access the average rates from the St. Louis Federal Reserve Banks' FRED database: Weekly Leaving the Board | Monthly Leaving the Board Leaving the Board Leaving the Board Back to top ##### Will discontinued series still be updated in the DDP? No. Historical rates that were previously on the H.15 release will continue to be available in the DDP, but they will no longer be updated. Back to top ##### How do I use the...

evidences.title: Understanding the Prime Rate: Definition, Calculation, and Impact
evidences.content: ## Factors Influencing Prime Rate Decisions The prime rate is determined by individual banks and used as the base rate for many types of loans, including loans to small businesses and credit cards. The Federal Reserve has no direct role in setting the prime rate, but most financial institutions choose to set their prime rates based partly on the target level of the federal funds rate established by the FOMC.3 One of the most used prime rates is the one that _The Wall Street Journal_ publishes daily. As noted above, banks generally use fed funds + 3 to determine the prime rate. In January 2025, the U.S. prime rate was 7.50%, lowered by 0.25% after the FOMC cut federal funds rates in December 2024.1 [...] Banks generally use the fed funds plus 3% to determine the current prime rate. The rate forms the basis for other interest rates, including rates for mortgages, small business loans, or personal loans. The prime rate changes in response to Federal Reserve policies aimed at controlling inflation and stimulating or moderating economic growth. Fixed-rate loans are not affected by changes in the prime rate. Article Sources [...] Video 1Image 7 ## What Is the Prime Interest Rate? The...

evidences.title: Understanding the Prime Rate: Definition, Calculation, and Impact
evidences.content: Banks generally use the fed funds plus 3% to determine the current prime rate. The rate forms the basis for other interest rates, including rates for mortgages, small business loans, or personal loans. The prime rate changes in response to Federal Reserve policies aimed at controlling inflation and stimulating or moderating economic growth. Fixed-rate loans are not affected by changes in the prime rate. Article Sources [...] The goal of the Federal Reserve is to encourage or discourage borrowing by businesses and consumers. Higher rates discourage borrowing while lower rates encourage it. ## What Was the Highest Prime Rate Ever Recorded in the United States? The highest prime rate ever recorded in the U.S. was 21.5%, which was reached in December 1980.6 ## The Bottom Line The prime rate is the interest rate charged by U.S. commercial banks to their most creditworthy customers and it's a benchmark for other loan rates, including mortgages and credit cards. It's based on the Federal Reserve's federal funds overnight rate. [...] Video 1Image 7 ## What Is the Prime Interest Rate? The prime rate is the interest rate banks charge their most trusted borrowers. Like all loan rates, the...

evidences.title: Understanding the Prime Rate: Definition, Calculation, and Impact
evidences.content: ## A Historical Overview of the Prime Rate The prime rate dates back to the 1930s when banks first used it to set interest rates for short-term lending to their most creditworthy customers following the Great Depression. In the decades following World War II, the prime rate remained relatively stable, hovering around 2% to 3%.6 The prime rate began to rise significantly in the 1970s as the United States experienced an economic recession and high inflation. The prime rate reached its all-time high of 21.5% in Dec. 1980, as the Federal Reserve sought to curb inflation by raising interest rates.6 [...] 5. The Wall Street Journal. "Money Rates." 6. Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, FRED. "Bank Prime Loan Rate Changes: Historical Dates of Changes and Rates (PRIME)." 7. Casaplorer. "Prime Rates from 1955 to February 2024." 8. Bank of America, Newsroom. "Prime Rate Information." evidences.title: Prime Rate | Definition & History | Study.com
evidences.content: # Prime Rate | Definition & History Learn about the prime rate and how it regulates much of the banking industry. Gain a deeper understanding of how the Federal Reserve impacts the prime rate. ## Table of Contents Prime Rate Definition Wall Street Prime Rate History Lesson Summary Show ### What has the prime rate been historically? The prime rate has been tracked since the mid-1940s. It has been as high at 21.50% in December 1980 and as low as, 1.75% in December 1947. ### What is the difference between the prime rate and the interest rate? The prime rate is the rate that a bank charges to its most creditworthy customers. Any additional interests rates that a bank sets for its loans and debt instruments are based upon its prime rate plus an additional percentage or points. [...] The federal funds rate is the amount banks charge for overnight funds transfers. It is the foundation upon which most banks build their prime rate. The federal funds rate is set by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) of the Federal Reserve. The FOMC meets about every six weeks or eight times during the calendar year. Most banks set their prime rate at 3% (300 basis points) above the federal funds rat...

evidences.title: Prime Rate - Prime Rate History From 1975 to 2025
evidences.content: HSH uses the print edition of the WSJ as the official source of the prime rate. Many (if not most) lenders specify this as their source of this index. Any bank can declare its own prime rate. Some smaller banks will use a larger bank's prime as a reference for pricing loans, but most use the Wall Street Journal version. ## When does the prime rate change? The prime rate does not change at regular intervals. It changes only when the nation's "largest banks" decide on the need to raise, or lower, their "base rate." The prime rate may not change for years, but it has also changed several times in a single year. Changes in the prime rate are highly correlated with changes in the federal funds rate. The prime rate typically changes a day or so after a change in the federal funds rate. evidences.title: What is the Prime Rate and How Does it Impact You | Citizens
evidences.content: The prime rate, in turn, is based on the federal funds rate. Also known as The Wall Street Journal prime rate or the U.S. Prime Rate, it’s a benchmark set and used by financial institutions to determine how much interest to charge a bank’s customers on loans. Typically, it’s about 3% higher than the federal funds rate. While the prime rate is likely the best rate available, it’s not a mandatory minimum for lenders to use. And just because the feds change the federal funds rate, financial institutions are not required to change their prime rate (although they often do). [...] ## So, what is the prime rate? Within the Federal Reserve, The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meets every six weeks to discuss and set the federal funds rate; sometimes it changes and sometimes it doesn’t. They look at the economy and other economic indicators to establish what they think will be a beneficial rate for banks to lend each other money. In slower economies, the FOMC tries to keep the federal funds rate low to encourage borrowing, which leads to spending and investing, but when the economy grows quickly, the FOMC might raise the rate to offset and balance the economy.
